card.intuition: Testing the quality of your tests by injecting tiny bugs (Mutants) into your source code and checking if your test suite catches them. If a test doesn't fail, the mutant 'Survived', and your tests are weak.
card.analogy: Testing a smoke alarm: you don't just check if the battery is in. You light a small match underneath it. If the alarm doesn't go off (The mutant survives), the alarm is broken, even if it has full batteries (High code coverage).
card.time_complexity: O(M * T) where M is mutants, T is tests (very slow!)
when_to_use: Evaluating whether '100% Code Coverage' is actually meaningful
Finding 'Useless' tests that pass even if the code logic is inverted
Highly critical systems (Aerospace, Medical) where missing an edge case is fatal
gotchas: Performance: it re-runs your entire test suite for EVERY mutant. For large apps, this can take hours.
Equivalent Mutants: some code changes don't actually change the behavior (e.g., changing `i++` to `i = i + 1`), which can lead to 'False Alarms'.

steps.label: Original tests
steps.annotation: Run existing test suite — all tests pass (green baseline).

steps.label: Generate mutants
steps.annotation: Mutation tool generates variants: flip operators, change values, remove lines.

steps.label: Run tests
steps.annotation: Full test suite re-run for each mutant.

steps.label: Killed
steps.annotation: Mutant killed: at least one test fails — tests are effective.

steps.label: Survived
steps.annotation: Mutant survives: no test caught the change — write a better test.

exercise.prompt: If a mutant is injected but no test in your suite fails, the mutant is said to have _________.
